rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is haemodynamically stable (BP maintained on standing, SpO2 normal), has presyncope but no true syncope, no chest pain and no tachycardia at rest — so this is not an emergency (not T4/T5). However, she is a severely symptomatic, effectively bedbound young person whose presentation requires an active stand test and an ECG to confirm a dysautonomia and, critically, to exclude a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia and significant hypovolaemia — none of which a telehealth service can perform. Because the dangerous mimic cannot be excluded remotely and the functional impairment is severe, the honest telehealth tier is an urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), not a routine 1-2 week wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Actual loss of consciousness (true fainting) or a fall
escalation_edges.rationale: True syncope shifts concern toward a dangerous arrhythmic or structural cardiac cause and warrants urgent emergency assessment rather than a routine pathway.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you actually black out or fall, call an ambulance or get to an emergency department straight away — that changes how urgently you need to be seen.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heart racing rapidly (>120 bpm) that persists for more than 30 minutes even while lying completely flat
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If sustained beyond 30 minutes despite lying flat
escalation_edges.rationale: A tachycardia not abolished by lying down suggests a primary arrhythmia rather than a postural dysautonomia and needs emergency evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heart keeps racing fast for more than half an hour even when you're lying flat, treat that as an emergency and seek urgent medical care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New chest pain, severe breathlessness, or inability to keep fluids down with signs of dehydration
escalation_edges.rationale: Chest pain or breathlessness broadens the cardiac differential; failure of oral intake with dehydration can drive dangerous instability — both warrant emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get chest pain, real trouble breathing, or you can't keep any fluids down and feel very unwell, go to an emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Diagnosis unconfirmable remotely in a severely symptomatic, bedbound patient
escalation_edges.rationale: The confirmatory active stand test and the arrhythmia-excluding ECG both require in-person assessment; a same-day face-to-face review is the correct baseline action.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because I can't do a standing blood-pressure test or an ECG over chat, I'd like you seen in person today for those checks while we start the fluid, salt and compression measures.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Parent is present and able to assist with transport and monitoring
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: A present carer makes a same-day in-person assessment feasible and provides a responsible adult to act on the syncope safety-netting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young adult (22) whose severe functional impairment (bedbound, unable to study) is disproportionate to any acute danger
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not let the benign-sounding label lower the urgency of the in-person work-up, nor let the anxiety and youth lead to dismissal; the disability itself justifies prompt assessment and active management.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like an autonomic (nervous-system) cause of your postural racing heart rather than a rhythm problem, but you should be seen in person today for a standing blood-pressure test and an ECG to be sure and to rule out a heart-rhythm cause before any heart medication is started. In the meantime, drink plenty of salty fluids, put compression tights on before getting out of bed, sit down to shower, and use leg-crossing/calf-tensing when you stand.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP or urgent care appointment; your parent can help arrange transport
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you develop any of the emergency warning signs (fainting, a racing heart that won't settle when lying flat, chest pain), call 000 or go to the nearest emergency department; otherwise book the in-person assessment first thing.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Share the wearable heart-rate readings (supine vs standing), the postural colour change and the timeline; the GP should perform an active stand test and ECG and arrange cardiology/autonomic referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as self-care or a routine 1-2 week problem and closing the case remotely — typically by anchoring on anxiety — without arranging the ECG/active-stand assessment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia is missed and mislabelled as anxiety/dysautonomia, leaving a treatable and potentially dangerous rhythm disorder undiagnosed while the patient remains bedbound.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: A prompt (24-48h) review is arranged but same-day in-person assessment is not, despite severe symptoms and an unexcluded arrhythmia.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a haemodynamically stable patient with chronic postural symptoms to an emergency ambulance (000), consuming emergency resources unnecessarily and alarming the patient.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the postural tachycardia is a benign dysautonomia (POTS) or a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia — unresolvable without an in-person ECG and active stand test.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: State the presumptive dysautonomia impression honestly, explain the remote limitation, and route the patient to a same-day in-person assessment for the confirmatory tests while starting safe conservative measures and giving clear safety-netting.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring a confirmed POTS diagnosis and a purely home plan, or collapsing the uncertainty onto 'anxiety' and prescribing an anxiolytic.
